coredata.dc:title: Highly fluorescent N doped C-dots as sensor for selective detection of Hg2+ in beverages
coredata.prism:publicationName: Spectrochimica Acta Part A: Molecular and Biomolecular Spectroscopy
coredata.dc:creator: Fu, Wen-Jing
Peng, Zhi-Xin
Dai, Ying
Yang, Yu-Fan
Song, Jia-Yu
Sun, Weiqing
Ding, Baomiao
Gu, Hui-Wen
Yin, Xiao-Li
coredata.dc:description: In this work, nitrogen doped carbon dots (NCDs) were synthesized through one step hydrothermal reaction using citric acid and ethylenediamine as precursors. The prepared NCDs exhibit high quantum yield of 67.4%, good stability, excellent selectivity and sensitivity. It was found that the NCDs have potential as a fluorescence sensor for the detection of Hg2+. Under optimal conditions, good linearity between the change in NCDs fluorescence intensity and Hg2+ concentration was obtained in the range of 0.3 to 2.0 μM with a detection limit at 0.24 μM. The possible detection mechanism was static quenching of NCDs by Hg2+. The established method was successfully applied to the determination of Hg2+ in beverage samples. The results indicated that the NCDs-based sensor has potential for detection of Hg2+ in real beverage sample.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Carbon dots
High quantum yield
Fluorescent probe
Hg2+
Beverages

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Synthesis and Characterization of the NCDs
Establishment of sensing model for Hg2+
Selectivity of NCDs fluorescence sensor for Hg2+ detection
Possible fluorescence quenching mechanism of NCDs

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Characterization of the NCDs. A. FT-IR spectra of NCDs; B. TEM image of NCDs (inset: particle size distribution and HRTEM image); C. UV–Vis absorption spectra of NCDs; D: Fluorescence spectra of NCDs.
Characterization. A. XPS survey spectrum of NCDs; B. High-resolution C1s spectrum of NCDs; C. High-resolution O1s spectrum of NCDs; D. High-resolution N1s spectrum of NCDs.
Sensitivity of NCDs for Hg2+ detection. A. FL spectra of NCDs in presence of different concentrations of Hg2+; B. Linear relationship between F/F0 and the Hg2+ concentration.

Selectivity of NCDs for Hg2+ detection. A, C and E. Fluorescence quenching of of various metal ions, amino acids and food additives towards NCDs; B, D and F. Interference of possible interfering substances on the determination sensing system for Hg2+ (10 μM).
Schematic illustration of the one step hydrothermal synthesis of NCDs and detection of Hg2+.
Performance comparison of the as-prepared N-CDs with other fluorescent probes towards Hg2+ detection.

ce:legend.ce:simple-para: Note: EDA: ethylenediamine; L-Cys: L-cysteine; CA: citric acid; TEA: triethylamine; FA: folic acid.

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Determination of Hg2+ in real samples.

ce:table-footnote.ce:label: b
ce:table-footnote.ce:note-para: RSD: Relative Standard Deviation = (Standard Deviation / Average) × 100%.

head.ce:title: Highly fluorescent N doped C-dots as sensor for selective detection of Hg2+ in beverages

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: College of Life Sciences, Yangtze University, Jingzhou 434025, China
ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: College of Life Sciences, Yangtze University, Jingzhou 434025, China

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: College of Chemistry and Environmental Engineering, Yangtze University, Jingzhou 434023, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: College of Chemistry and Environmental Engineering

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: College of Chemistry and Environmental Engineering, Yangtze University, Jingzhou 434023, China

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: In this work, nitrogen doped carbon dots (NCDs) were synthesized through one step hydrothermal reaction using citric acid and ethylenediamine as precursors. The prepared NCDs exhibit high quantum yield of 67.4%, good stability, excellent selectivity and sensitivity. It was found that the NCDs have potential as a fluorescence sensor for the detection of Hg2+. Under optimal conditions, good linearity between the change in NCDs fluorescence intensity and Hg2+ concentration was obtained in the range of 0.3 to 2.0 μM with a detection limit at 0.24 μM. The possible detection mechanism was static quenching of NCDs by Hg2+. The established method was successfully applied to the determination of Hg2+ in beverage samples. The results indicated that the NCDs-based sensor has potential for detection of Hg2+ in real beverage sample.

ce:section.ce:para: With the rapid development of technology and economy, toxic heavy metal ions pollution has given rise on a global scale, which is attributed to agricultural and industrial waste disposal [1,2]. Among them, mercury ion (Hg2+) is one of the most prevalent and dangerous heavy metal ions, which can cause serious harm to both human health and environment due to it is bioaccumulation and high toxicity [3,4]. There is a direct correlation between exposure to Hg2+ and the consumption of Hg-contaminated aquatic products, agricultural products and other food products. Exposure to Hg2+ can damage the nervous system and digestive system and lead to kidney failures and death [5,6]. Therefore, it is essential to develop a rapid, selective and sensitive response method for the detection of Hg2+.

Up to now, numerous techniques, such as atomic absorption spectroscopy (AAS) [7], inductively coupled plasma atomic emission spectrometry (ICP-AES) [8] and inductively coupled plasma mass spectrum (ICP-MS) [9], have been applied to detect Hg2+ in various samples. These inspection methods require high-level professional expertise, tedious and time-consuming procedures. In comparison, fluorescent sensors have garnered considerable attention in the field of metal ions detection [10–15], bio-imaging [16,17] due to their advantages of rapid response, high sensitivity and convenience. At present, based on organic fluorescent dye, quantum dots (QDs) and metal nanoparticles, various fluorescent sensors have been developed for metal ions detection [18–20]. For example, He’s group studied a triarylamine-based fluorescent covalent organic framework for efficient detection and removal of Hg2+ [20]. However, the above fluorescent probes have disadvantages such as toxicity, low sensitivity, low selectivity and poor hydrophilicity, which limit their practical applications [21].

As a new type of carbon-based nanomaterial, carbon dots (CDs), have attracted great interest in the research field of sensing, bioimaging, photocatalysis, etc. [22–26], due to their excellent fluorescence properties, favorable biocompatibility, high stability, environmental friendliness and low-cost synthetic strategy. There are a few sensors based on CDs applied to detect heavy metal ions [27–31]. For example, He et al. designed a functional preserving carbon dots-based fluorescent probe for Hg2+ sensing in herbal medicines via coordination and electron transfer [32]. Li et al. prepared a guanine decorated carbon dots based on a domestic microwave oven for detection of Ag+ in the real samples [33]. However, most of them do not provide excellent photoluminescence (PL) quantum yield (QY) and selectivity, which limits the practical application of CDs. Therefore, CDs with attributes of low cost, high photoluminescent and selectivity are urgently needed.

Synthetic methods to prepare CDs were divided into two categories of top-down and bottom-up [34]. Compared with top-down method, the bottom-up method provides us more privileges to tune CDs with well-defined sizes, versatile surface states and interior structures by regulating the molecular structures of precursors and synthesis processes. Doping was a commonly used way of controlling their chemical, optical, and electrical properties. The introduction of hetero-atoms (B, N, P, S, etc.) can not only improve the fluorescence properties of CDs [35], but also give CDs-based sensors with high selectivity for specific ions [36,37]. In this work, a highly selective method for Hg2+ detection has been developed via nitrogen doping fluorescent CDs (NCDs), which is prepared by hydrothermal method using citric acid and ethylenediamine as precursors. The quantum yield of synthesized NCDs is as high as 67.4%. Its fluorescence can be obviously quenched by low concentrations of Hg2+. Moreover, a number of metal ions and commonly used food additives in beverages were used as interferences to fully investigate the selectivity of NCDs. Furthermore, the NCDs were applied to detect Hg2+ in real beverage samples. The principle of Hg2+ detection is illustrated in Scheme 1 .

All chemicals used in the experiment were of analytical grade. Ethylenediamine (EDA) was obtained from Beilian Fine Chemicals Development Co., Ltd (Tianjin, China), while the citric acid (CA), metal ion salts FeCl3·6H2O, CaCl2, KCl, MgCl2·6H2O, MnCl2·4H2O, NiCl2·6H2O and quinine sulfate were purchased from Hushi Laboratory Equipment Co., Ltd (Shanghai, China), CdN2O6·4H2O, ZnCl2, Cu(NO3)2 were purchased from Macklin Biochemical Technology Co., Ltd (Shanghai, China) and Pb(NO3)2, AgNO3, HgCl2 were purchased from Guizhou Tongren Chemical Reagent Factory (Guizhou, China). The source of amino acids, acidity agents, sweetening agents, umami enhancer, vitamin, pigments, preservatives and ethanol were shown in Table S1. All chemicals were used as received without further purification. The beverages were obtained from local supermarket (Jingzhou, China) and tap water was obtained from laboratory. All solutions are prepared with deionized water (resistivity = 18.25 MΩ cm) from an ULUPURE ultrapure water system (Chengdu, China).

Transmission electron microscopy (TEM) images were obtained on a HT-7800 (Hitachi, Tokyo, Japan) operating at 100 kV. The high resolution TEM (HRTEM) was measured under the Tecnai G2 F20 (FEI, USA) operating at 200 kV. The fluorescence (FL) spectra were recorded with an F-4600 FL spectrophotometer (Hitachi, Tokyo, Japan). The fluorescence spectra were scanned at an excitation wavelength of 350 nm, and the emission spectral range was 400–600 nm. The emission slit and the excitation slit width were both set to 10 nm. The photo-multiplier tube (PMT) voltage was set at 500 V. The fluorescence lifetime was measured with an FLS1000 spectrophotometer (Edinburgh, UK). The UV–Vis absorption spectra were recorded with a TU-1900 spectrophotometer (Beijing, China). Fourier transform infrared (FT-IR) spectra were acquired on a Ruian FT-IR-680 Spectrometer (Tianjin, China). Raman spectra were measured with a Renishaw Raman system (Renishaw, UK) with radiation at 488 nm. X-ray Photoelectron Spectroscopy (XPS) was investigated by using ESCALAB 250Xi spectrometer with a mono X-Ray source Mg Kα excitation. Binding energy calibration was based on C1s at 284.6 eV. X-ray diffraction (XRD) pattern was obtained on Bruker D8 Advance (Bruker, Germany) with a wavelength of λ = 0.15406 nm. Zeta potentials were measured using a Malverm Nano ZS90 (Malverm, UK).

The water-soluble fluorescent NCDs were prepared by a one-step hydrothermal reaction using citric acid and ethylenediamine as precursors. The synthesis process was referred to [38] and modified appropriately, which can be described as follows: citric acid (2 g) and ethylenediamine (0.954 mL) were dissolved in 20 mL deionized water via sonication. Then the solution was carefully transferred to a poly tetrafluoroethylene (Teflon)-lined autoclave (25 mL) and heated at 200 °C for 8 h. After the reaction was finished, the reactor was cooled to room temperature naturally. The obtained solution was filtered with a 0.22 μm of mixed cellulose esters (MCE) membrane to remove large aggregates. Finally, the fresh NCDs solution was diluted 10, 0000 times and stored at 4 °C for following experiments. The NCDs powder was obtained through freeze-drying method.

Quantum yields (QY) were measured according to reported literature [38]. Quinine sulfate in 0.1 M H2SO4 (as solvent) were chosen as standards (QY = 0.54 at 350 nm). The QY of CDs in water (as solvent) was calculated according by the following equation (1):
(1)
Q
N C D s
=
Q
st
I
NCDs
I
st
A
st
A
NCDs
η
NCDs
2
η
st
2
where Q denotes the QY, Ι is the measured emission intensity, A is the absorbance at the excitation wavelength, and η is the refractive index of the solvent. The subscript “NCDs” and “st” refers to the NCDs sample and standard sample, respectively. For these aqueous solutions, ηNCDs /ηst = 1. In order to minimize reabsorption effects, absorbance was kept below 0.05 at the excitation wavelength of 350 nm.

All the detection of Hg2+ was investigated at room temperature. In a typical experiment, 1 mL PBS buffer (0.1 M, pH = 7.0) and 0.2 mL NCDs solution were added into a 2.0 mL tube, followed by the addition of different concentrations of the Hg2+ and then diluted to 2.0 mL with deionized water. Fluorescence scanning was performed after mixed well. The selectivity of the NCDs was verified by replacing Hg2+ with other metal ions, amino acids and commonly used additives in beverages (including acidity agents, sweetening agents, umami enhancer, vitamin, pigments, preservatives and ethanol). The interference experiment was performed by adding metal ions, amino acids and commonly used additives as interferents when detecting Hg2+. The concentration of Hg2+ was 10 μM, and the concentration of metal ion salts was 20 times that of Hg2+. The concentration of amino acids, acidity agents, sweetening agents, umami enhancer, vitamin, pigments, preservatives and ethanol were 10 times that of Hg2+.

Real samples (tap water, Drinking water, and beverages) were put into 50 mL beaker, ultrasound for 10 min, and then heated and boiled for 10 min. After cool down to room temperature, the solutions were filtered through a 0.22 μm of MCE membrane. Then, 0.5 mL pre-analyzed sample was spiked with Hg2+ at three concentration levels (0.3, 0.8 and 1.5 μM). Three replicate experiments were performed at each spiked concentration level. The detection procedure was similar to the standard detection procedure for Hg2+.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Synthesis and Characterization of the NCDs
ce:section.ce:para: Nitrogen doping is a common and effective method for modulating the intrinsic properties of C-dots. The introduction of nitrogen impurities contributes excess electrons into C-dots, which results in an upward shift of the Fermi level and a change in optical properties [35]. Nitrogen doping can effectively improve the fluorescence quantum yield of CDs, and increase with the increase of doping amount. But the larger the amount of ethylenediamine, the higher the viscosity of the prepared NCDs, and the longer it takes to dry, which is disadvantageous for practical application [38]. Thus, in this study, the molar ratio of ethylenediamine and citric acid was about 1:1, and the prepared NCDs with a quantum yield of 67.4% were obtained.

The FT-IR spectra of NCDs were acquired to study the functional groups of NCDs. As shown in Fig. 1
A, a broad absorption peak located at 3410–3200 cm−1 corresponding to stretching vibration of O-H/N-H, a strong peak centered at 1662 cm−1 was attributed to characteristic absorption bands of CO stretching vibrations, indicating the existence of oxygen and carbon-containing functional groups. The peaks at 1487 cm−1 and 1395 cm−1 are ascribed to characteristic absorption bands of CC and CH stretching vibrations, respectively. In addition, C-N stretching vibrations or N-H bending vibration at 1558 cm−1 can also be observed, which illustrated the existence of secondary amide group [17,18]. Moreover, the surface groups were also characterized by XPS analysis, Fig. 2 A indicates three very typical peaks, C1s (285.07 eV), N1s (399.37 eV) and O1s (532.57 eV), existed in the synthetic NCDs. The elemental composition (atomic %) of N-CDs was C 47.93%, N 9.61%, and O 42.46%, demonstrating that N-CDs were nitrogen-doped due to the addition of EDA. In Fig. 2B the high-resolution XPS spectra of C1s proved the existence of C-N (287.97 eV), CO (287.07 eV), C-O (285.97 eV), and CC/CC (284.45 eV) bonds. Fig. 2C (high-resolution O1s spectra) exhibited the presences of C-O (532.62 eV) and CO (530.81 eV) bonds. Fig. 2D (high-resolution N1s spectra) showed the presence of C-N (399.4 eV) bond. The XPS spectra revealed the successful doping of nitrogen atoms in NCDs. These data demonstrated that the C-dots were surrounded by COOH and/or NH2 groups, which were consistent with the results reported by Du et al. [39] previously.

The morphology and microstructure of the NCDs were identified by the typical TEM image. As displayed in the TEM image (Fig. 1B), the as-prepared NCDs was uniform in shape with an average diameter of 16.4 nm, resulting in the fluorescence of NCDs was excitation-independent [40]. In addition, obvious crystalline lattice fringes with spacing of 0.33 nm were observed in high-resolution TEM image, which verified the existence of sp2-hybridized carbon in NCDs. Raman spectra ( Fig. S1
A) of NCDs show two distinct peaks at 1350 cm−1 (D-band) and 1588 cm−1 (G-band), respectively. The D band is related to vibrations of carbon atoms with dangling bonds in the termination plane of disordered graphite or glassy carbon. The G band represents the E2g mode of graphite and is associated with the vibration of sp2-hybridized carbon atoms in a two-dimensional hexagonal lattice. The intensity ratio of the D band to the G band was found to be 1.71, indicating that they have high contents of sp3 carbon atoms [40]. The XRD patterns of the NCDs ( Fig. S1
B) showed a sharp peak centered at 2θ = 26.79° with an interlayer spacing distance of 3.32 Å, which is consistent with the results of HRTEM results. These results demonstrated that NCDs contain a partial amorphous structure, which is partially attributed to the existence of nitrogen doping defects [41].

As shown in Fig. 1C, the NCDs solution is colorless and transparent under white light, and displayed strong blue fluorescence under excitation with a UV lamp at 365 nm. Two obvious absorption peaks at 240 nm and 350 nm were observed, which may be attributed to the π-π* transition of CC/CN bond and n-π* transition of CO bond, respectively [42]. As displayed in Fig. S2 , the fluorescence excitation wavelength of carbon dots are range from 280 to 400 nm, and emission wavelength are range from 430 to 600 nm. The maximum fluorescent excitation wavelength was at 350 nm, corresponding to a strongest fluorescent emission peak at 445 nm (Fig. 1D), which is consistent with UV–Vis absorption spectra results. With the increase of excitation wavelength from 320 to 380 nm, the emission peak position has not changed significantly, indicating excitation independent emission. The results demonstrated that the NCDs surface was relatively uniform and well-passivated, which is identical to the TEM results. Quantum yields (QY) were measured by UV–Vis and FL spectrophotometer, and the QY was 67.4%. Compared to those previously reported CDs without doping of nitrogen atoms, these NCDs had a higher QY. The result was listed in Table 1 .

As we all know, the fluorescence stability of the NCDs can be affected by various factors such as pH, ionic strengths etc. Therefore, several tests were carried out to investigate the fluorescence stability of the NCDs. Firstly, the effect of pH (1.0–12.0) on fluorescence intensity of NCDs was investigated and displayed in Fig. S3
A. With the pH varies from 1.0 to 5.0, the fluorescence intensity of the NCDs increases significantly but remain stable in the pH range of 5.0 to 9.0, and then decreases significantly in the pH range of 9.0 to 12.0. The increase of fluorescence intensity in acidic environment may be attributed to the protonation process of carboxylate on surface of NCDs. By that analogy, the stable fluorescence intensity of NCDs in a neutral environment probably correspond to the protonation equilibrium process [32]. The deprotonation of carboxylate groups on the surface of NCDs in an alkaline environment lead to a gradual decrease in fluorescence intensity. Therefore, a pH of 7.0 was selected for detection of Hg2+.

Furthermore, the influence of ionic strengths on the fluorescence intensity of NCDs was studied. The ionic strengths were controlled by different concentrations of KCl. Excellent ionic strengths stability of the NCDs can be observed when the concentration of KCl was in the range of 0–2 M ( Fig. S3
B), which ensured their wide applications in various complicated conditions. The fluorescence intensity of carbon dots at different temperatures was also measured. Fig. S3
C shows the temperature stability of the NCDs. Finally, the stability of NCDs with storage time was studied ( Fig. S3
D). It was found that fluorescence intensity were stable during storage for up to 14 days. These results demonstrated that the NCDs have great potential for sensing applications in complex analytical systems.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Establishment of sensing model for Hg2+
ce:section.ce:para:
Based on the conditions mentioned above, the capability of NCDs for quantitative detection of Hg2+ was evaluated. As shown in Fig. 3 A, after the addition of Hg2+, the fluorescence intensity of the NCDs solution at 445 nm was strongly quenched without any blue shift or red shifts, revealing that Hg2+ can efficiently quench the fluorescence of NCDs. After the addition of Hg2+, the fluorescence intensity of the NCDs can reach stability within 2 min ( Fig. S4
A and S4B), indicating that the binding of Hg2+ to the NCDs is fast. The fluorescence intensity of NCDs at 445 nm gradually decreased as the Hg2+ concentration increased. And when the concentration of Hg2+ was added up to 10 μM, the FL intensity can be considered quenched completely. A good linear relationship was obtained between the F/F0 values and the Hg2+ concentration in a range from 0.3 to 2.0 μM (Fig. 3B). The linearity was fitted with the Stern-Volmer equation, and it can be expressed as F/F 0 = -0.1755x + 0.7929 (R 2 = 0.9981). The detection limit (LOD) was 0.24 μM, which is obtained based on 3 times standard deviation/slope. As can be seen in Table 1, it is apparent that the proposed fluoresence sensor has satisfactory results in LOD compared to those previously reported in literature [11,12,27–29,43,44].

ce:section.ce:section-title: Selectivity of NCDs fluorescence sensor for Hg2+ detection
ce:section.ce:para: It’s well known that high selectivity is essential for Hg2+ detection in real samples. Therefore, experiments were performed to study the selectivity and anti-interference capability of NCDs fluorescence sensor. The selectivity of NCDs on various common metal ions (including Hg2+, Fe3+, K+, Mn2+, Ni2+, Cd2+, Zn2+, Cu2+, Pb2+, Ag+) with the concentration of 200 μM were studied. As shown in Fig. 4 A, it is clear that only Hg2+ causes an obviously decrease in the fluorescence emission of NCDs. The detection of Hg2+ by the NCDs was unaffected by the presence of other metal ions (Fig. 4B).

Moreover, the influence of several common food additives of beverage (including amino acids, acidity agents, sweetening agents, umami enhancer, vitamin, pigments preservatives and ethanol) on the selectivity of Hg2+ sensor were investigated. As displayed in Fig. 4C, D, E and F, almost no significant change in the fluorescence intensity of NCDs was discovered in the presence of various food additives (100 μM). While an efficient fluorescence quenching of NCDs by Hg2+ was observed even in the presence of the above interferents. It can be concluded that interfering constituents have no obvious impact on the detection of Hg2+. These phenomenon providing evidence that the NCDs have excellent selectivity and good anti-interference capability.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Possible fluorescence quenching mechanism of NCDs
ce:section.ce:para: Fluorescence quenching is a common sensing principle of metals detection based upon CDs. It can be classified into two categories, i.e. static quenching (ground state complex formation) and dynamic quenching (collisional). Static and dynamic quenching can be distinguished by their differing dependence on temperature, or preferably by lifetime measurements [45]. Specifically, the lifetime of CDs is one of the most useful methods to distinguish static or dynamic quenching. As for dynamic quenching, the quencher diffuses to the fluorophore in excited state and returns to the ground state without emission of a photon, in which an equivalent decrease in fluorescence intensity and lifetime occurs. In static quenching, a nonfluorescent ground state complex formation occurs between fluorophore and quencher, in which the fluorescence lifetime of the intrinsic fluorophore is not modified.

The fluorescence decay curves of NCDs aqueous suspension in the absence and presence of Hg2+ (10 μM) were shown in Fig. S5
A. The fluorescence lifetime is calculated to be 11.99 ns without Hg2+ and 11.45 ns in the presence of Hg2+ [46], which is nearly invariable, indicating the occurrence of static quenching and formation of ground state complex [47]. While the UV − Vis spectra ( Fig. S5
B) of the N-CDs solution clearly show a red shift after addition of Hg2+, which also implied the formation of ground state complex. Studies have shown that carboxylic groups can bind with metal ions by metal–ligand interaction [34,48]. Carboxylic groups surrounded on the surface of carbon dots may result in the metal–ligand interaction between CDs and Hg2+, thus causing the fluorescence quenching and the UV–Vis spectrum red shift of the NCDs in the presence of Hg2+. The recognition process was illustrated in Scheme 1.

In order to further confirm the quenching mechanism between N-CDs and Hg2+, the effect of temperature on Stern-Volmer curve was studied. The Stern-Volmer equation is as follows:
(2)
F 0
F
= 1 +
K sv
[ Q ]
where F 0 is the fluorescence of NCDs, F is the fluorescence of NCDs in the presence Hg2+, K SV refers to Stern-Volmer constant, Q denotes the balance concentrations of Hg2+. As for static quenching, higher temperature will typically result in the dissociation of weakly bound complexes, and hence smaller amounts of static quenching [45]. Fig.S5
C and Table S2 shows that Ksv decrease with rising temperature, indicating static quenching. Additionally, zeta potential was measured to further explore the detection mechanism. The zeta potential of Hg2+ was −3.28 mV. And the zeta potential of NCDs in PBS reduced from −14.35 mV to −10.85 mV after Hg2+ was added. That means the change of surface charge of NCDs, due to the interaction with Hg2+ ions. As a result, the stability of the NCDs is lost and thus leads to the aggregation of the NCDs. The TEM image shows the NCDs were aggregated in the presence of Hg2+ ( Fig. S5
D). These phenomena providing evidence that complex formation induces the NCDs aggregated [32]. These results indicated that the fluorescence quenching of NCD by Hg2+ is a static quenching mechanism [49,50].

The results of this study show that the NCDs-based fluorescent sensor for Hg2+ detection has advantages of high selectivity, rapid response and simple operation. In order to test whether this NCDs-based sensor system is capable of being used in the quantification of Hg2+ in real samples, two water samples and three beverage samples were used to evaluate the developed NCDs sensor. As presented in Table 2 , no Hg2+ was detected in all real samples. The recovery of the Hg2+ at three spiked concentration levels ranged from 90.42% to 110.89% with relative standard deviation (RSD) in the range of 0.52% to 7.56%. The recovery results obtained in this study demonstrated that the proposed sensor has strong anti-interference ability and can be applied to selective detection of Hg2+ in different beverage samples.

In summary, NCDs were successfully synthesized via one step hydrothermal treatment of CA and EDA. The as-prepared NCDs displayed good photostability and fluorescence quantum yield, as high as 67.4%. The fluorescence NCDs was efficiently quenched by Hg2+ due to the formation of NCDs-Hg2+ complexes, which may be attribute to the metal–ligand interaction between carboxylic groups and metal ions. The possible detection mechanism was static quenching of NCDs by Hg2+. A good linearity between quenched fluorescence intensity and concentration of Hg2+ was obtained in a range of 0.3 to 2.0 μM with a detection limit at 0.24 μM. The interference experiments of the NCDs-based sensor carried out in the presence of other metal ions and food additives, revealing an excellent selectivity for Hg2+ detection. What’s more, when this sensor applied for detection of Hg2+ in real samples, acceptable recoveries (90.42–110.89%) and RSDs (0.52–7.56%) values were obtained, indicating the good accuracy, high sensitivity and selectivity of the proposed detection strategy. These findings demonstrate the application prospect of NCDs-based sensor in monitoring trace amounts of Hg2+ in real food products.

Wen-Jing Fu: Conceptualization, Methodology, Writing – original draft. Zhi-Xin Peng: Methodology, Data curation, Visualization. Ying Dai: Methodology, Data curation, Visualization. Yu-Fan Yang: Methodology, Data curation, Visualization. Jia-Yu Song: Data curation, Visualization, Writing – review & editing. Weiqing Sun: Supervision, Writing – review & editing. Baomiao Ding: Writing – review & editing. Hui-Wen Gu: Funding acquisition, Investigation, Software, Writing – review & editing. Xiao-Li Yin: Funding acquisition, Project administration, Writing – review & editing.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: The authors would like to acknowledge the financial supports from the National Natural Science Foundation of China (Grant Nos. 32001790 and 31701693), the Natural Science Foundation of Hubei Province (Grant No. 2018CFB165), and the College Students' Innovative Entrepreneurial Training Plan Program of Yangtze University (Grant No. Yz2020267).

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data to this article can be found online at https://doi.org/10.1016/j.saa.2021.120392.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para:
The following are the Supplementary data to this article:
Supplementary data 1

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Rationally engineered nanosensors: A novel strategy for the detection of heavy metal ions in the environment
J. Hazard. Mater.
